title: Bread and butter pudding
subtitle: British baked custard pudding with layered bread and currants
excerpt: Sliced white bread buttered generously, layered in a dish with currants and a pinch of nutmeg, covered with a vanilla custard, and baked until the top is golden and the custard is just set. A British kitchen staple that transforms day-old bread.
sourceNotes: Bread and butter pudding is one of the oldest documented British baked puddings. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, records a 'bread and butter pudding' using thin-sliced buttered bread, currants, eggs, milk, and sugar, baked in a moderate oven. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, includes a similar preparation under 'baked bread pudding'. The recipe here follows the Beeton formula: white bread, butter, currants, a well-seasoned egg custard, and a moderate bake.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble the dish and pour the custard up to 2 hours ahead; leave to soak in the fridge, then bake when needed.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, in a roasting tin half-filled with hot water (bain-marie). Fan at 150°C. The water bath prevents the custard from curdling at the edges while the centre sets. Without the bath, the edges overcook and weep before the centre is done.

glossaryTerms.definition: A roasting tin filled with enough hot water to come halfway up the sides of the pudding dish, placed in the oven alongside the dish. The water moderates the temperature around the dish, preventing the custard from reaching the 85°C threshold at which eggs scramble. A correctly baked custard wobbles as one mass in the centre when the dish is nudged; it sets further as it cools.

content.text: Bread and butter pudding is at its best when the bread is a day old, the butter is generous, and the custard is rich. The

is not fussy: a roasting tin with boiling water up to the halfway mark of the pudding dish. It is the single step that prevents the edges from curdling while the centre sets. The top layer of bread bakes to a satisfying golden crust above the custard line; below, the bread softens into the cream.

attrs.prepNote: plus extra to scatter on top

content.text: Butter the bread on one side and cut each slice into triangles. Butter a shallow baking dish of about 1.5 litres capacity. Lay in a layer of bread triangles, buttered side up and slightly overlapping. Scatter over half the currants and a third of the sugar. Add a second layer of bread, sprinkle with the remaining currants and another third of the sugar. Finish with a top layer of bread, points up, which will crisp as it bakes.

Whisk together the eggs, milk, cream, vanilla, remaining sugar, and nutmeg. Pour slowly over the bread, making sure all the bread is moistened. Press the top layer gently into the custard. Leave to soak for at least 30 minutes — the bread absorbs the custard and the top layer will be just above the surface. Scatter a little extra caster sugar over the top.

Heat the oven to 170°C conventional. Set the pudding dish in a deep roasting tin and pour in enough boiling water to come halfway up the sides of the dish. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the top is deep gold and the custard is set at the edges but wobbles gently in the centre when the dish is nudged. Remove from the water bath and leave to settle for 5 minutes before serving. Serve with cold double cream poured alongside.
